title: Meta-free few-shot learning via representation learning with weight averaging

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - - - META FREE FEW SHOT LEARNING VIA REPRESENTA TION LEARNING WITH WEIGHT AVERAGING

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Recent studies on few-shot classification using transfer learning pose challenges to the effectiveness and efficiency of episodic meta-learning algorithms. Transfer learning approaches are a natural alternative, but they are restricted to few-shot classification. Moreover, little attention has been on the development of probabilistic models with well-calibrated uncertainty from few-shot samples, except for some Bayesian episodic learning algorithms. To tackle the aforementioned issues, we propose a new transfer learning method to obtain accurate and reliable models for few-shot regression and classification. The resulting method does not require episodic meta-learning and is called meta-free representation learning (MFRL). MFRL first finds low-rank representation generalizing well on meta-test tasks. Given the learned representation, probabilistic linear models are fine-tuned with few-shot samples to obtain models with well-calibrated uncertainty. The proposed method not only achieves the highest accuracy on a wide range of few-shot learning benchmark datasets but also correctly quantifies the prediction uncertainty. In addition, weight averaging and temperature scaling are effective in improving the accuracy and reliability of few-shot learning in existing meta-learning algorithms with a wide range of learning paradigms and model architectures.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Currently, the vast majority of few-shot learning methods are within the general paradigm of metalearning (a.k.a. learning to learn) (Bengio et al., 1991; Schmidhuber, 1987; Thrun & Pratt, 1998), which learns multiple tasks in an episodic manner to distill transferrable knowledge (Vinyals et al., 2016; Finn et al., 2017; Snell et al., 2017). Although many episodic meta-learning methods report state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance, recent studies show that simple transfer learning methods with fixed embeddings (Chen et al., 2019; Tian et al., 2020) can achieve similar or better performance in few-shot learning. It is found that the effectiveness of optimization-based meta-learning algorithms is due to reusing high-quality representation, instead of rapid learning of task-specific representation (Raghu et al., 2020). The quality of the presentation is not quantitatively defined, except for some empirical case studies (Goldblum et al., 2020). Recent machine learning theories (Saunshi et al., 2021) indicate that low-rank representation leads to better sample efficiency in learning a new task. However, those theoretical studies are within the paradigm of meta-learning and do not reveal how to obtain low-rank representation for few-shot learning outside the realm of meta-learning. This motivates us to investigate ways to improve the representation for adapting to new few-shot tasks in a meta-free manner by taking the advantage of simplicity and robustness in transfer learning.

In parallel, existing transfer learning methods also have limitations. That is, the existing transfer learning methods may not find representation generalizing well to unseen few-shot tasks (Chen et al., 2019; Dhillon et al., 2020) , compared with state-of-the-art meta-learning methods (Ye et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020). Although some transfer learning methods utilize knowledge distillation and self-supervised training to achieve strong performance in few-shot classification, they are restricted to few-shot classification problems (Mangla et al., 2020; Tian et al., 2020). To the best of our knowledge, no transfer learning method is developed to achieve similar performance to metalearning in few-shot regression. As such, it is desirable to have a transfer learning method that finds high-quality representation generalizing well to unseen classification and regression problems.

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

The last limitation of the existing transfer learning methods in few-shot learning is the lack of uncertainty calibration. Uncertainty quantification is concerned with the quantification of how likely certain outcomes are. Despite a plethora of few-shot learning methods (in fact, machine learning in general) to improve the point estimation accuracy, few methods are developed to get probabilistic models with improved uncertainty calibration by integrating Bayesian learning into episodic metatraining (Grant et al., 2018; Finn et al., 2018; Yoon et al., 2018; Snell & Zemel, 2021). Few-shot learning models can be used in risk-averse applications such as medical diagnosis (Prabhu et al., 2019). The diagnosis decision is made on not only point estimation but also probabilities associated with the prediction. The risk of making wrong decisions is significant when using uncalibrated models (Begoli et al., 2019). Thus, the development of proper fine-tuning steps to achieve well-calibrated models is the key towards practical applications of transfer learning in few-shot learning.

In this paper, we develop a simple transfer learning method as our own baseline to allow easy regularization towards more generalizable representation and calibration of prediction uncertainty. The regularization in the proposed transfer learning method works for regression and classification problems so that we can handle both problems within a common architecture. The calibration procedure is easily integrated into the developed transfer learning method to obtain few-shot learning models with good uncertainty quantification. Therefore, the resulting method, called Meta-Free Representation Learning (MFRL), overcomes the aforementioned limitations in existing transfer learning methods for few-shot learning. Our empirical studies demonstrate that the relatively overlooked transfer learning method can achieve high accuracy and well-calibrated uncertainty in few-shot learning when it is combined with the proper regularization and calibration. Those two tools are also portable to meta-learning methods to improve accuracy and calibration, but the improvement is less significant compared with that of transfer learning.

We use stochastic weight averaging (SWA) (Izmailov et al., 2018), which is agnostic to loss function types, as implicit regularization to improve the generalization capability of the representation. We also shed light on that the effectiveness of SWA is due to its bias towards low-rank representation. To address the issue of uncertainty quantification, we fine-tune appropriate linear layers during the meta-test phase to get models with well-calibrated uncertainty. In MFRL, hierarchical Bayesian linear models are used to properly capture the uncertainty from very limited training samples in few-shot regression, whereas the softmax output is scaled with a temperature parameter to make the few-shot classification model well-calibrated. Our method is the first one to achieve well-calibrated few-shot models by only fine-tuning probabilistic linear models in the meta-test phase, without any learning mechanisms related to the meta-training or representation learning phase.

Our contributions in this work are summarized as follows:

- We propose a transfer learning method that can handle both few-shot regression _and_ classification problems with performance exceeding SOTA.
- For the first time, we empirically find the implicit regularization of SWA towards low-rank representation, which is a useful property in transferring to few-shot tasks.
- The proposed method results in well-calibrated uncertainty in few-shot learning models while preserving SOTA accuracy.
- The implicit regularization of SWA and temperature scaling factor can be applied to existing meta-learning methods to improve their accuracy and reliability in few-shot learning.

## 2 RELATED WORK

**Episodic meta-learning** approaches can be categorized into metric-based and optimization-based methods. Metric-based methods project input data to feature vectors through nonlinear embeddings and compare their similarity to make the prediction. Examples of similarity metrics include the weighted _L_ 1 metric (Koch et al., 2015), cosine similarity (Qi et al., 2018; Vinyals et al., 2016), and Euclidean distance to class-mean representation (Snell et al., 2017). Instead of relying on predefined metrics, learnable similarity metrics are introduced to improve the few-shot classification performance (Oreshkin et al., 2018; Sung et al., 2018). Recent metric-based approaches focus on developing task-adaptive embeddings to improve few-shot classification accuracy. Those task-adaptive embeddings include attention mechanisms for feature transformation (Fei et al., 2021; Gidaris & Komodakis, 2018; Ye et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2021), graph neural networks (Garcia & Estrach,

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

2018), implicit class representation (Ravichandran et al., 2019), and task-dependent conditioning (Oreshkin et al., 2018; Yoon et al., 2020; 2019). Although metric-based approaches achieve strong performance in few-shot classification, they cannot be directly applied to regression problems.

Optimization-based meta-learning approaches try to find transferrable knowledge and adapt to new tasks quickly. An elegant and powerful meta-learning approach, termed model-agnostic metalearning (MAML), solves a bi-level optimization problem to find good initialization of model parameters (Finn et al., 2017). However, MAML has a variety of issues, such as sensitivity to neural network architectures, instability during training, arduous hyperparameter tuning, and high computational cost. On this basis, some follow-up methods have been developed to simplify, stabilize and improve the training process of MAML (Antoniou et al., 2018; Flennerhag et al., 2020; Lee & Choi, 2018; Nichol et al., 2018; Park & Oliva, 2019). In practice, it is very challenging to learn high-dimensional model parameters in a low-data regime. Latent embedding optimization (LEO) attempts to learn low-dimensional representation to generate high-dimensional model parameters (Rusu et al., 2019). Meanwhile, R2-D2 (Bertinetto et al., 2019) and MetaOptNet (Lee et al., 2019) reduce the dimensionality of trainable model parameters by freezing feature extraction layers during inner loop optimization. Note that the proposed method is fundamentally different from R2-D2 and MetaOptNet because our method requires neither episodic meta-learning nor bi-level optimization.

**Transfer learning** approaches first learn a feature extractor on all training data through standard supervised learning, and then fine-tune a linear predictor on top of the learned feature extractor in a new task (Chen et al., 2019). However, vanilla transfer learning methods for few-shot learning do not take extra steps to make the learned representation generalizing well to unseen meta-test tasks. Some approaches in this paradigm are developed to improve the quality of representation and boost the accuracy of few-shot classification, including cooperative ensembles (Dvornik et al., 2019), knowledge distillation (Tian et al., 2020), and auxiliary self-supervised learning (Mangla et al., 2020). Nevertheless, those transfer learning methods are restricted to few-shot classification. MFRL aims to find representation generalizing well from the perspective of low-rank representation learning, which is supported by recent theoretical studies (Saunshi et al., 2021). Furthermore, MFLR is the first transfer learning method that can handle both few-shot regression and classification problems and make predictions with well-calibrated uncertainty.

## 3 BACKGROUND

## 3.1 EPISODIC META-LEARNING

In episodic meta-learning, the meta-training data contains _T_ episodes or tasks, where the _τ[th]_ episode consists of data _Dτ_ = _{_ ( **x** _τ,j,_ **y** _τ,j_ ) _}j[N]_ =1 _[τ]_[with] _[ N][τ]_[samples. Tasks and episodes are used interchange-] ably in the rest of the paper. Episodic meta-learning algorithms aim to find common model parameters _θ_ which can be quickly adapted to task-specific parameters _φτ_ ( _τ_ = 1 _, ..., T_ ). For example, MAML-type algorithms assume _φτ_ is one or a few gradient steps away from _θ_ (Finn et al., 2017; 2018; Grant et al., 2018; Yoon et al., 2018), while other meta-learning approaches assume that _φτ_ and _θ_ share the parameters in the feature extractor and only differ in the top layer (Bertinetto et al., 2019; Lee et al., 2019; Snell et al., 2017).

## 3.2 STOCHASTIC WEIGHT AVERAGING

The idea of stochastic weight averaging (SWA) along the trajectory of SGD goes back to Polyak–Ruppert averaging (Polyak & Juditsky, 1992). Theoretically, weight averaging results in faster convergence for linear models in supervised learning and reinforcement learning(Bach & Moulines, 2013; Lakshminarayanan & Szepesvari, 2018). In deep learning, we are more interested in tail stochastic weight averaging (Jain et al., 2018), which averages the weights after _T_ training epochs. The averaged model parameters _θ_ SWA can be computed by running _s_ additional training epochs using SGD

## 4 METHODOLOGY

The proposed method is a two-step learning algorithm: meta-free representation learning followed by fine-tuning. We employ SWA to make the learned representation low-rank and better generalize to meta-test data. Given a meta-test task, a new top layer is fine-tuned with few-shot samples to obtain probabilistic models with well-calibrated uncertainty. Note that MFRL can be used for both regression and classification depending on the loss function. The pseudocode of MFRL is presented in Appendix A.1.

A Hessian matrix with a lot of small eigenvalues corresponds to a flat minimum, where the loss function is less sensitive to the perturbation of model parameters (Keskar et al., 2017). It is known that averaging the weights after SGD convergence in a local minimum basin pushes _θT_ towards the flat side of the loss valley (He et al., 2019). As a result, SWA could result in a faster decay of eigenvalues in the kernel matrix, and thus low-rank representation. Our conjecture about SWA as implicit regularization towards low-rank representation is empirically verified in Section 5.

## 4.2 FINE-TUNING

Given the learned representation, we train a new probabilistic top layer in a meta-test task using fewshot samples. The new top layer will be configured differently depending on whether the few-shot task is a regression or a classification problem.

**In a few-shot classification task** , a new logistic regression model is learned with the post-processed representation. It is difficult to select an appropriate value of _λ_ in each of the meta-test tasks due to the lack of validation data in _D_ . We instead treat _λ_ as a global hyper-parameter so that the value of _λ_ should be determined based on the accuracy on meta-validation data. Note that the selected _λ_ with high validation accuracy does not necessarily lead to well calibrated classification models. As such, we introduce the temperature scaling factor (Guo et al., 2017) as another global hyper-parameter to

where _T_ is the temperature scaling factor. In practice, we select the L2 regularization coefficient _λ_ and the temperature scaling factor _T_ as follows. At first, we set _T_ to 1, and do grid search on the meta-validation data to find the _λ_ resulting in the highest meta-validation accuracy. However, finetuning _λ_ does not ensure good calibration. It is the temperature scaling factor that ensures the good uncertainty calibration. Similarly, we do grid search of _T_ on the meta-validation set, and choose the temperature scaling factor resulting in the lowest expected calibration error (Guo et al., 2017). Note that different values of _T_ do not affect the classification accuracy because temperature scaling is accuracy preserving.

## 5 EXPERIMENTS

We follow the standard setup in few-shot learning literature. The model is trained on a meta-training dataset and hyper-parameters are selected based on the performance on a meta-validation dataset. The final performance of the model is evaluated on a meta-test dataset. The proposed method is applied to few-shot regression and classification problems and compared against a wide range of alternative methods.

## 5.1 FEW-SHOT REGRESSION RESULTS

Sine waves (Finn et al., 2017) and head pose estimation (Patacchiola et al., 2020) datasets are used to evaluate the performance of MFRL in few-shot regression. We use the same backbones in literature (Patacchiola et al., 2020) to make fair comparisons. Details of the few-shot regression experiments can be found in Appendix A.2.

The results for few-shot regression are summarized in Table 1. In the sine wave few-shot regression, MFRL outperforms all meta-learning methods, demonstrating that high-quality representation can be learned in supervised learning, without episodic meta-learning. Although DKT with a spectral mixture (SM) kernel achieves high accuracy, the good performance should be attributed to the strong inductive bias to periodic functions in the SM kernel (Wilson & Adams, 2013). Additional results for MFRL with different activation functions are reported in Appendix A.3. In the head pose estimation experiment, MFRL also achieves the best accuracy. In both few-shot regression problems, SWA results in improved accuracy, suggesting that SWA can improve the quality of features and facilitate the learning of downstream tasks. In Fig. 1, uncertainty is correctly estimated by the hierarchical Bayesian linear model with learned features using just 10 training samples.

## 5.2 FEW-SHOT CLASSIFICATION RESULTS

We conduct few-shot classification experiments on four widely used few-shot image recognition benchmarks: miniImageNet (Ravi & Larochelle, 2017), tieredImageNet (Ren et al., 2018), CIFARFS (Bertinetto et al., 2019), and FC100 (Oreshkin et al., 2018). In addition, we test our approach on a cross-domain few-shot classification task from the miniImageNet to CUB. The experiment details about the few-shot classification datasets can be found in Appendix A.2. The proposed method is applied to three widely used network architectures: ResNet-12 (Lee et al., 2019; Ravichandran et al., 2019), wide ResNet (WRN-28-10) (Dhillon et al., 2020; Rusu et al., 2019), and a 4-layer convolutional neural network with 64 channels (Chen et al., 2019; Patacchiola et al., 2020) (in Appendix A.3).

The results of the proposed method and previous SOTA methods using similar backbones are reported in Table 2 and 3. The proposed method achieves the best performance in most of the experiments when compared with previous SOTA methods. Our method is closely related to Baseline++ (Chen et al., 2019) and fine-tuning on logits (Dhillon et al., 2020). Baseline++ normalizes both classification weights and features, while the proposed method only normalizes features. It allows our method to find a more accurate model in a more flexible hypothesis space, given high-quality representation. Compared with fine-tuning on logits, our method obtains better results by learning

7

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

a new logistic regression model on features, which store richer information about the data. Some approaches pretrain a _C_ -class classification model on all training data and then apply highly sophisticated meta-learning techniques to the pretrained model to achieve SOTA performance (Rusu et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2019). Our approach with SWA outperforms those pretrained-then-meta-learned models, which demonstrates that SWA obtains high-quality representation that generalizes well to unseen tasks. Compared with improving representation quality for few-shot classification via selfdistillation (Tian et al., 2020), the computational cost of SWA is significantly smaller because it does not require training models from scratch multiple times. Moreover, SWA can be applied to find good representation for both few-shot regression and classification, while previous transfer learning approaches can only handle few-shot classification problems (Mangla et al., 2020; Tian et al., 2020).

MFRL is also applied to the cross-domain few-shot classification task as summarized in Table 4. MFRL outperforms other methods in this challenging task, indicating that the learned representation has strong generalization capability. We use the same hyperparameters (training epochs, learning rate, learning rate in SWA, SWA epoch, etc.) as in Table 2. The strong results indicate that MFRL is robust to hyperparameter choice. Surprisingly, meta-learning methods with adaptive embeddings do not outperform simple transfer learning methods like Baseline++ when the domain gap between base classes and novel classes is large. We notice that Tian et al. (2020) also reports similar results that transfer learning methods show superior performance on a large-scale cross-domain few-shot classification dataset. We still believe that adaptive embeddings should be helpful when the domain gap between base and novel classes is large. Nevertheless, how to properly train a model to obtain useful adaptive embeddings in novel tasks is an open question.

## 5.3 EFFECTIVE RANK OF THE REPRESENTATION

The rank of representation defines the number of independent bases. For deep learning, noise in gradients and numerical imprecision can cause the resulting matrix to be full-rank. Therefore, simply counting the number of non-zero singular values may not be an effective way to measure the rank of the representation. To compare the effective ranks, we plot the normalized singular values of the representation of meta-test data in Fig. 2, where the representation with SWA has a faster decay in singular values, thus indicating the lower effective rank of the presentation with SWA. The results empirically verify our conjecture that SWA is an implicit regularizer towards low-rank representation.

Figure 3: Study on the temperature scaling factor for 5-way 5-shot classification using ResNet-12 for the proposed MFRL and existing episodic meta learning methods. Uncalibrated models are in the first row, and calibrated models with the temperature scaling factor are in the second row.

## 5.4 FEW-SHOT CLASSIFICATION RELIABILITY

The proposed method not only achieves high accuracy in few-shot classification but also makes the classification uncertainty well-calibrated. A reliability diagram can be used to check model calibration visually, which plots an identity function between prediction accuracy and confidence when the model is perfectly calibrated (DeGroot & Fienberg, 1983). Fig. 3 shows the classification reliability diagrams along with widely used metrics for uncertainty calibration, including expected calibration error (ECE) (Guo et al., 2017), maximum calibration error (MCE) (Naeini et al., 2015), and Brier score (BRI) (Brier, 1950). ECE measures the average binned difference between confidence and accuracy, while MCE measures the maximum difference. BRI is the squared error between the predicted probabilities and one-hot labels. MAML is over-confident because tuning a deep neural network on few-shot data is prone to over-fitting. Meanwhile, Proto Net and Matching Net are better calibrated than MAML because they do not fine-tune the entire network during testing. Nevertheless, they are still slightly over-confident. The results indicate that MFRL with a global temperature scaling factor can learn well-calibrated models from very limited training samples.

## 5.5 APPLICATION IN META-LEARNING

Meanwhile, we also apply SWA to episodic meta-learning methods, such Proto Net, MAML and Matching Net, to improve their classification accuracy. The results in Table 5 indicate that SWA can improve the few-shot classification accuracy in both transfer learning and episodic meta-learning. SWA is orthogonal to the learning paradigm and model architecture. Thus, SWA can be applied to a wide range of few-shot learning methods to improve accuracy.

Furthermore, the temperature scaling factor can be applied to calibrate meta-learning methods, including MAML, Proto Net, and Matching Net. The reliability diagrams in Fig. 3 indicate that the temperature scaling factor not only calibrates classification uncertainty of transfer learning approaches, such as the proposed MFRL, but also makes the classification uncertainty well-calibrated in episodic meta-learning methods. Therefore, the temperature scaling factor can be applied to a wide range of few-shot classification methods to get well-calibrated uncertainty, while preserving the classification accuracy.

9

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## 6 DISCUSSION

SWA has been applied to supervised learning of deep neural networks (Izmailov et al., 2018; Athiwaratkun et al., 2019) and its effectiveness was attributed to convergence to a solution on the flat side of an asymmetric loss valley (He et al., 2019). However, it does not explain the effectiveness of SWA in few-shot learning because the meta-training and meta-testing losses are not comparable after the top layer is retrained by the few-shot support data in a meta-test task. The effectiveness of SWA in few-shot learning must be related to the property of the representation. Although our results empirically demonstrate that SWA results in low-rank representation, further research about their connection is needed.

Explicit regularizers can also be used to obtain simple input-output functions in deep neural networks and low-rank representation, including L1 regularization, nuclear norm, spectral norm, and Frobenius norm (Bartlett et al., 2017; Neyshabur et al., 2018; Sanyal et al., 2020). However, some of those explicit regularizers are not compatible with standard SGD training or are computationally expensive. In addition, it is difficult to choose the appropriate strength of explicit regularization. Too strong explicit regularization can bias towards simple solutions that do not fit the data. In comparison, SWA is an implicit regularizer that is completely compatible with the standard SGD training without much extra computational cost. Thus, it can be easily combined with transfer learning and meta-learning to obtain more accurate few-shot learning models. In parallel, SWA is also robust to the choice of the hyperparameters - the learning rate and training epochs in the SWA stage (see details in Appendix A.4).

## 7 CONCLUSIONS

In this article, we propose MFRL to obtain accurate and reliable few-shot learning models. SWA is an implicit regularizer towards low-rank representation, which generalizes well to unseen meta-test tasks. The proposed method can be applied to both classification and regression tasks. Extensive experiments show that our method not only outperforms other SOTA methods on various datasets but also correctly quantifies the uncertainty in prediction.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a compelling transfer learning framework for few-shot learning that unifies regression and classification under a meta-free paradigm, leveraging stochastic weight averaging (SWA) to improve representation quality and uncertainty calibration. It makes a strong empirical case across multiple benchmarks and provides useful ablation studies on SWA and temperature scaling. The integration of hierarchical Bayesian modeling for regression and temperature scaling for classification is principled and practical, and the claim that SWA biases representations toward lower effective rank is supported by singular value analysis.

Overview issues:

- Inadequate comparison with strong meta-learning baselines in few-shot regression: The paper claims superiority over episodic meta-learning methods in few-shot regression, but the baselines used—MAML, Bayesian MAML, ALPaCA, R2D2, and DKT—are either outdated or not state-of-the-art for regression tasks. Notably absent are comparisons with modern probabilistic meta-learning approaches such as PLATIPUS or meta-learning variants of Gaussian processes that are specifically designed for uncertainty-aware few-shot regression. The DKT+Spectral result, while strong, is attributed to inductive bias rather than dismissed as a competitive baseline; the paper should instead compare against methods that also use flexible kernels without task-specific priors. Without such comparisons, the claim that MFRL 'outperforms all meta-learning methods' is overstated and potentially misleading.

- Ambiguity in the application of SWA within the representation learning phase: The paper states that SWA is applied post-SGD convergence (Eq. 1), but it does not clarify whether this averaging is performed over multiple independent runs, training epochs, or fine-tuned variants. Section 4.1 suggests averaging over epochs after convergence, yet the experimental setup (Appendix A.1, not shown) may imply a different protocol. This ambiguity affects reproducibility and interpretation: if SWA is applied only once per training run, its regularization effect may be minor compared to ensembling. Moreover, the ablation in Table 1 shows a large gain from SWA, but without clarification of the averaging scope, it is unclear whether this gain comes from better optimization or implicit ensembling. The method must specify whether weights are averaged across epochs of a single run or across multiple retrainings.

- Lack of ablation on the role of SWA in uncertainty calibration: While the paper shows that SWA improves accuracy and representation rank, it does not isolate its effect on uncertainty calibration. The fine-tuning phase uses temperature scaling for classification and Bayesian inference for regression—both of which dominate the calibration behavior. The reliability diagrams in Fig. 3 compare calibrated vs. uncalibrated models but do not disentangle whether SWA itself contributes to better-calibrated uncertainty beyond what temperature scaling achieves. A critical ablation would compare MFRL with and without SWA *using the same temperature scaling factor*, to determine if SWA leads to more stable or better-calibrated posteriors. Without this, the claim that SWA contributes to 'reliable models' is not fully substantiated.

- Overlooked sensitivity of hierarchical Bayesian regression to few-shot data: The paper assumes that the hierarchical Bayesian linear model in the regression fine-tuning stage reliably estimates hyperparameters (λ, σ²) from very few samples (e.g., 10-shot). However, with n << p, such estimation is known to be unstable and highly sensitive to initialization and optimization dynamics. The method uses iterative optimization (Tipping, 2001), but no evidence is provided that the solution is robust across random seeds or data splits. Given that the entire uncertainty quantification rests on this step, the lack of error bars on predicted variance (only a single value in Fig. 1) is concerning. The paper should report variance of uncertainty estimates or perform posterior predictive checks to validate calibration in regression.

- Unsupported claim that low-rank representations directly cause better few-shot generalization: The paper posits that SWA improves performance by inducing low-rank representations, citing theoretical work (Saunshi et al., 2021) and showing faster singular value decay (Fig. 2). However, no causal link is established: it is possible that SWA improves generalization through flatter minima or better optimization, independent of rank. A more rigorous test would involve comparing SWA with explicit low-rank regularizers (e.g., nuclear norm) or controlling for rank while varying other factors. Without such controls, the interpretation remains correlational. The discussion acknowledges this gap but presents the low-rank hypothesis as more established than the evidence supports.

- No demonstration that low-rank representations cause improved performance: The paper attributes MFRL's success to SWA-induced low-rank representations but does not establish a causal link. It shows singular values decay faster with SWA (Fig. 2), but this correlation alone doesn't prove that rank reduction drives generalization. A natural test would be to compare SWA against an explicit low-rank regularizer—such as nuclear norm penalty or PCA-based projection—on the same backbone and tasks. If SWA outperforms these direct rank controls, the implicit regularization claim gains support. Without such a comparison, the mechanism remains speculative, and readers cannot distinguish whether performance stems from rank or other SWA effects like flat minima.

- Missing ablation on SWA's contribution to uncertainty calibration: The paper claims MFRL produces well-calibrated uncertainty, but the reliability gains may come entirely from temperature scaling and Bayesian inference rather than SWA. To isolate SWA's role, the authors should report calibration metrics (ECE, Brier score) for MFRL both with and without SWA, using the same temperature scaling factor selected on the meta-validation set. If calibration improves only when SWA is paired with proper scaling, that supports synergy; if calibration is unchanged, then SWA contributes little. This ablation is essential to justify the claim that SWA enhances reliability beyond accuracy gains.

- No evaluation on standard few-shot regression baselines with uncertainty quantification: The paper claims superior uncertainty quantification in few-shot regression but compares only point accuracy (MSE) against methods like ALPaCA and DKT. It omits comparisons on proper uncertainty metrics such as negative log-likelihood, calibration error, or coverage of prediction intervals. Standard benchmarks like PLATIPUS or meta-learning with Gaussian processes are designed to provide calibrated uncertainty in regression and should be included. Without such comparisons, the claim that MFRL achieves 'reliable' models lacks empirical grounding in the regression setting.

- Unclear scope of weight averaging in SWA implementation: The method describes SWA as averaging weights over epochs after convergence (Eq. 1), but it does not specify whether this occurs within a single run or across multiple independent training runs. This ambiguity affects reproducibility and interpretation: if averaging spans multiple retrainings, it introduces ensembling effects beyond standard SWA. The ablation in Table 1 shows large gains from SWA, but without clarifying the averaging protocol, it is unclear whether the improvement comes from optimization smoothing or model averaging. The paper should explicitly state whether weights are averaged over epochs of one run or across runs, and confirm this in the appendix.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Include comparisons with modern probabilistic meta-learning baselines for few-shot regression, particularly those with calibrated uncertainty.

- Clarify the implementation of SWA—specifically, whether weight averaging is across epochs, runs, or models—and ensure reproducibility by detailing the averaging window and frequency.

- Add an ablation study isolating the effect of SWA on uncertainty calibration, independent of temperature scaling and Bayesian fine-tuning.

- Provide evidence of robustness in hierarchical Bayesian regression fine-tuning, including variance of uncertainty estimates and sensitivity to initialization.

- Temper the causal interpretation of low-rank representations by either adding controlled experiments or reframing the claim as a hypothesis supported by correlational evidence.

comments.title: Unsupported claim that SWA induces low-rank representations
comments.quote: SWA is an implicit regularizer towards low-rank representation, which generalizes well to unseen meta-test tasks.
comments.explanation: The paper asserts that SWA acts as an implicit regularizer toward low-rank representations, but this claim is not substantiated by a mechanistic explanation or ablation study. While Figure 2 shows faster singular value decay under SWA, no evidence isolates rank reduction as the cause of improved generalization. Alternative effects—such as flatter minima or implicit ensembling—could explain the gains. The discussion in Section 6 acknowledges the lack of theoretical justification, yet the conclusion presents the link as established. To avoid overstatement, rephrase to reflect that SWA is empirically associated with lower-rank structure and better performance, but the causal mechanism remains open.

comments.title: Overstated claim of outperforming SOTA without full baseline coverage
comments.quote: Extensive experiments show that our method not only outperforms other SOTA methods on various datasets but also correctly quantifies the uncertainty in prediction.
comments.explanation: The claim of outperforming 'other SOTA methods' is not fully supported. For few-shot regression, the baselines exclude modern probabilistic approaches like PLATIPUS or meta-GPs that are designed for uncertainty-aware learning. Moreover, uncertainty metrics (e.g., NLL, calibration error) are not reported for regression, making it impossible to validate the claim of 'correctly quantifies' in that setting. The comparison is limited to point accuracy against methods with strong inductive biases (e.g., DKT+Spectral), yet their performance is dismissed rather than fairly benchmarked. Temper the claim to reflect competitive performance within the evaluated scope, rather than broad superiority.

comments.title: Misleading attribution of DKT+Spectral's performance to inductive bias
comments.quote: Although DKT with a spectral mixture (SM) kernel achieves high accuracy, the good performance should be attributed to the strong inductive bias to periodic functions in the SM kernel *(Wilson and Adams, 2013)*.
comments.explanation: The paper downplays DKT+Spectral's strong performance by attributing it solely to inductive bias, implying it is less general or less impressive than MFRL. However, this reasoning is selectively applied: MFRL also benefits from architectural and training biases (e.g., SWA, Bayesian linear head), yet these are not framed as limiting. The SM kernel’s ability to model periodicity is a valid and useful inductive bias, not a flaw. To maintain objectivity, revise the statement to acknowledge DKT+Spectral’s strength while highlighting MFRL’s advantage in not requiring task-specific kernel design.

comments.title: Incorrect effective rank metric invalidates spectral analysis
comments.quote: The metric  $-\sum \bar{\sigma}_i\log \bar{\sigma}_i$  is used to measure the effective rank of the representation, where  $\bar{\sigma}_i = \sigma_i / \sigma_{\mathrm{max}}$ . Faster decay in singular values indicates that fewer dimensions capture the most variation in all dimensions, thus lower effective rank.
comments.explanation: The quantity $-\sum \bar{\sigma}_i \log \bar{\sigma}_i$ with $\bar{\sigma}_i = \sigma_i / \sigma_{\mathrm{max}}$ is not a valid entropy because $\bar{\sigma}_i$ do not sum to one and are not a probability distribution. The standard effective rank uses $\tilde{\sigma}_i = \sigma_i / \sum_j \sigma_j$ and is defined as $\exp(-\sum \tilde{\sigma}_i \log \tilde{\sigma}_i)$. The paper's metric lacks a coherent interpretation—for example, it gives zero for any matrix with equal singular values, regardless of dimension. This undermines the empirical verification of low-rank structure in Section 5.3. Replace the metric with the standard definition or justify its use with a formal argument.

comments.title: Normalization by max singular value invalidates rank comparison
comments.quote: To compare the effective ranks, we plot the normalized singular values of the representation of meta-test data in Fig. 2, where the representation with SWA has a faster decay in singular values, thus indicating the lower effective rank of the presentation with SWA.
comments.explanation: Normalizing singular values by $\sigma_{\mathrm{max}}$ preserves decay shape but discards total spectral mass, making it unsuitable for comparing effective rank. Two matrices can have identical normalized spectra but vastly different nuclear norms, leading to different effective ranks under standard definitions. For instance, a matrix with one large singular value and many small ones may appear similar to a more diffuse spectrum after normalization, despite having lower effective dimensionality. The conclusion that SWA reduces effective rank cannot be supported without plotting cumulative explained variance or computing the proper effective rank. Revise the analysis to use a valid normalization scheme.

comments.title: Missing specification of representation matrix dimensions
comments.quote: To compare the effective ranks, we plot the normalized singular values of the representation of meta-test data in Fig. 2, where the representation with SWA has a faster decay in singular values, thus indicating the lower effective rank of the presentation with SWA.
comments.explanation: The paper does not specify the shape or construction of the representation matrix whose singular values are analyzed. Is it $N \times d$ (samples × features) or $d \times N$? This matters because the singular values and effective rank depend on the matrix aspect ratio. If $N \ll d$, the rank is artificially capped at $N$, regardless of model properties. Without clarifying how the representation is formed (e.g., batch size, number of classes), the analysis lacks reproducibility. Add a sentence specifying the matrix dimensions and data sampling procedure to ensure the comparison is meaningful.

comments.title: Unjustified use of point estimates in predictive distribution
comments.quote:
The predictive distribution for a new sample $\mathbf{x}_{*}$ is

$p\left(y_{*}\mid\mathbf{x}_{*},\mathbf{X},\mathbf{y},\lambda,\sigma^{2}\right)=\int p\left(y_{*}\mid\mathbf{x}_{*},\mathbf{w},\sigma^{2}\right)p\left(\mathbf{w}\mid\mathbf{X},\mathbf{y},\lambda,\sigma^{2}\right)d\mathbf{w},$ (4)
comments.explanation: Equation (4) conditions the predictive distribution on point estimates of $\lambda$ and $\sigma^2$, ignoring uncertainty in hyperparameters. In few-shot settings with limited data, these estimates are highly uncertain, and failing to marginalize over them underestimates predictive variance. A fully Bayesian treatment would require integrating over $p(\lambda, \sigma^2 \mid \mathbf{X}, \mathbf{y})$. While computational efficiency may justify this approximation, it contradicts the claim of 'well-calibrated uncertainty'. Acknowledge this as a limitation by adding: 'Note that this predictive distribution conditions on point estimates; full uncertainty quantification would require marginalization over hyperparameters.'

comments.title: Misrepresentation of RVM inference procedure
comments.quote: The iterative optimization based approximate inference *(Tipping, 2001)* is chosen because it is highly efficient. The point estimation for $\lambda$ and $\sigma^{2}$ is obtained by maximizing the marginal likelihood function $p\left(\mathbf{y}\mid\mathbf{X},\lambda,\sigma^{2}\right)$. The posterior of model parameters $p\left(\mathbf{w}\mid\mathbf{X},\mathbf{y},\lambda,\sigma^{2}\right)$ is calculated using the estimated $\lambda$ and $\sigma^{2}$. Previous two steps are repeated alternately until convergence.
comments.explanation: The described procedure alternates between estimating hyperparameters and computing the posterior over $\mathbf{w}$, which risks data reuse and overfitting. In the standard RVM framework (Tipping, 2001), hyperparameters are estimated by maximizing the marginal likelihood $\log p(\mathbf{y} \mid \mathbf{X}, \lambda, \sigma^2)$ with $\mathbf{w}$ analytically integrated out—no posterior on $\mathbf{w}$ is computed during optimization. The paper's description suggests a flawed iterative scheme that conditions hyperparameter updates on a posterior already using the data. Correct the text to reflect that $\lambda$ and $\sigma^2$ are estimated via type-II maximum likelihood with $\mathbf{w}$ marginalized, not alternated with posterior computation.

comments.title: Ambiguity in multi-task regression setup
comments.quote:
)

For regression problems, the model learns $\mathcal{T}$ regression tasks ($\mathbf{W}=[\mathbf{w}_{1},...,\mathbf{w}_{\mathcal{T}}]\in\mathbb{R}^{(p+1)\times\mathcal{T}}$) simultaneously using the loss function $\mathcal{L}_{MSE}$ given in Eq.
comments.explanation: The model assumes task-specific output heads $\mathbf{w}_\tau$, but the training procedure does not specify how task identity is encoded or how gradients are routed. Without task identifiers in the input or a mechanism to select the correct head during SGD, all $\mathbf{w}_\tau$ will be updated on every batch, effectively collapsing to a single-output model. This contradicts the $\mathbb{R}^{(p+1)\times\mathcal{T}}$ parameterization. Clarify whether task embeddings are used or reformulate as a multi-output regression with a single $\mathcal{T}$-dimensional head to ensure the setup is reproducible and logically consistent.

comments.title: Overstated Hessian approximation condition
comments.quote:
nearized networks with squared loss, the Gauss-Newton matrix $G$

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

approximates the Hessian matrix $H$ when $y$ is well-described by $f_{\theta}(x)$ *(Martens, 2020)*. This is the case when SGD converges to $\theta_{T}$ within a local minimum basin. A Hessia
comments.explanation: The Gauss-Newton matrix $G$ equals only the second term of the Hessian $H$; the first term involving second derivatives of $f_\theta$ vanishes only if residuals and curvature are both small. Convergence to a local minimum ensures small residuals but not small curvature—sharp minima can still have large second-order terms. The paper's claim that convergence implies $G \approx H$ is therefore incorrect. Revise to state that the approximation holds when both residuals and network curvature are small, or cite the precise conditions under which it is valid.

comments.title: Incorrect eigenvalue equivalence claim
comments.quote: In parallel, $K^{\mathrm{NTK}}$ shares the same eigenvalues of the Gauss-Newton matrix $G=\frac{1}{N^{\prime}}\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{X})^{\top}\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{X})$.
comments.explanation: While $K^{\mathrm{NTK}} = \mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^\top$ and $G = \frac{1}{N'} \mathbf{J}^\top\mathbf{J}$ share the same non-zero eigenvalues, their full spectra differ unless $N' = |\theta|$. The paper's phrasing suggests full spectral equivalence, which is false in general. Moreover, the claim that $K^{\mathrm{NTK}}$ and $K^{\mathrm{C}} = \Phi\Phi^\top$ have similar eigenvalue distributions lacks justification—$K^{\mathrm{NTK}}$ includes gradients from all layers, while $K^{\mathrm{C}}$ depends only on last-layer features. Remove the eigenvalue equivalence claim and either cite a specific result for spectral similarity or remove that assertion.

comments.title: Missing justification for flat hyperpriors in few-shot setting
comments.quote: The hyper-priors become very flat and non-informative when $a$, $b$, $c$ and $d$ are set to very small values.
comments.explanation: Setting Gamma hyperpriors with small shape and rate parameters (e.g., $\epsilon \to 0$) is not truly non-informative and can bias inference—$\mathrm{Gamma}(\lambda \mid \epsilon, \epsilon)$ places high density near zero, favoring large $\lambda$ (strong regularization), which may underfit in few-shot tasks. In low-data regimes, such priors can dominate the likelihood and lead to improper posteriors. The authors should justify their choice (e.g., $a = b = c = d = 10^{-4}$) by showing it stabilizes inference without unduly influencing results. Add: 'We use weakly informative priors to stabilize estimation while minimizing influence on the posterior.'

comments.title: Overconfident claim about calibration without ablation
comments.quote: The results indicate that MFRL with a global temperature scaling factor can learn well-calibrated models from very limited training samples.
comments.explanation: The claim attributes good calibration to MFRL, but calibration is primarily achieved via post-hoc temperature scaling—a standard recalibration technique applied after training. The paper does not show that the base MFRL model (without scaling) is well-calibrated. To support the claim, report calibration metrics (ECE, Brier score) for MFRL both with and without temperature scaling. Otherwise, the improvement may stem entirely from scaling, not from representation learning or SWA. Revise to clarify that calibration is achieved *via* temperature scaling applied to MFRL outputs, not *by* MFRL alone.

comments.title: SWA applied to MAML without algorithmic justification
comments.quote: Meanwhile, we also apply SWA to episodic meta-learning methods, such Proto Net, MAML and Matching Net, to improve their classification accuracy.
comments.explanation: The paper applies SWA to MAML without explaining how weight averaging integrates with its nested optimization structure. MAML updates meta-parameters based on task-specific inner-loop gradients; averaging meta-parameters across epochs could disrupt this dependency. It is unclear whether SWA is applied to meta-parameters, adapted parameters, or some combination. In contrast, SWA is well-defined for static training objectives like transfer learning. Without clarification, the procedure is ambiguous and potentially invalid. Specify exactly which weights are averaged and over which training stages, or remove MAML from the SWA application list.

comments.title: Overgeneralization of SWA's orthogonality claim
comments.quote: SWA is orthogonal to the learning paradigm and model architecture. Thus, SWA can be applied to a wide range of few-shot learning methods to improve accuracy.
comments.explanation: The claim that SWA is 'orthogonal' to learning paradigm overreaches the evidence. The paper only tests SWA on gradient-based methods (ProtoNet, MAML, MatchingNet) and shows inconsistent gains (e.g., +0.68% on MatchingNet 1-shot). It does not apply SWA to non-gradient-based methods (e.g., nearest-neighbor classifiers) where no persistent weight trajectory exists. The effectiveness of SWA depends on training dynamics and may not generalize across paradigms. Revise to acknowledge that SWA helps in certain gradient-based settings but its broader applicability remains unproven.

comments.title: Incomplete sentence breaks logical argument
comments.quote: Compared with fine-tuning on logits, our method obtains better results by learning
comments.explanation: The sentence is cut off mid-phrase, breaking the logical flow and leaving a key claim unresolved. The intended point—likely about learning a well-calibrated classifier on improved representations—is not completed, undermining the argument for MFRL's superiority. This appears to be a formatting error. Rewrite the sentence in full, e.g., 'by learning a new logistic regression model on a more transferable, low-rank feature space induced by SWA.'

comments.title: Redundant 'where' disrupts equation flow
comments.quote:
where

<!-- PAGE BREAK -->

where $\theta_{i}$ denotes the model parameters at the end of the $i$-th epoch.
comments.explanation: The repetition of 'where' creates a syntactic break that interrupts the logical connection between equation (1) and its definition. The first 'where' serves no purpose and should be removed to maintain a clean, linear flow from equation to explanation. Rewrite by removing the first 'where' to ensure a single, coherent definition follows the equation.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes Meta-Free Representation Learning (MFRL) as an alternative to episodic meta-learning for few-shot tasks. While the method shows impressive empirical results across multiple benchmarks, there are several high-level concerns that need to be addressed regarding the research design, methodological choices, and the framing of contributions.

The paper makes a valuable contribution by demonstrating that simple transfer learning with weight averaging can outperform complex meta-learning methods. The empirical results are comprehensive and the uncertainty quantification aspect is novel for this paradigm. However, the paper suffers from insufficient theoretical justification for its core claims, inadequate analysis of failure cases and domain shift scenarios, and questions about the novelty of combining existing techniques.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient theoretical justification for SWA's effectiveness: Section 4.1 provides only a conjecture about SWA leading to low-rank representations through connections to NTK and Hessian matrices. The paper lacks rigorous theoretical analysis to support this claim. The argument relies on empirical eigenvalue decay shown in Figure 2, but this does not establish causality. Without a solid theoretical foundation, the claim that SWA's effectiveness comes from low-rank regularization remains speculative. This is particularly important given that the paper positions this as a key insight. A proper theoretical analysis or at least more extensive ablation studies would strengthen this claim significantly.

- Limited analysis of domain shift and failure cases: The paper includes only one cross-domain experiment (miniImageNet to CUB in Table 4) but does not systematically analyze performance under varying degrees of domain shift. The literature context mentions that transfer methods struggle with significant domain changes, yet the paper does not address this limitation adequately. There is no discussion of scenarios where MFRL might fail or underperform compared to meta-learning methods. This omission is significant because practical few-shot learning applications often involve substantial domain shifts. The paper should include more diverse cross-domain experiments and analyze failure modes to provide a complete picture of the method's limitations.

- Questionable novelty of the proposed approach: The paper frames MFRL as a novel method, but it primarily combines existing techniques: standard transfer learning, SWA (from Izmailov et al., 2018), hierarchical Bayesian linear regression (from Tipping, 2001), and temperature scaling (from Guo et al., 2017). The paper does not sufficiently distinguish its contribution from simply applying these known techniques to few-shot learning. The claim that MFRL is "the first transfer learning method that can handle both few-shot regression and classification problems" (Section 1) needs verification against the literature. The paper should more clearly articulate what is truly novel about the combination and integration of these components.

- Inadequate uncertainty calibration analysis for regression: While the paper demonstrates uncertainty quantification for regression in Figure 1, it lacks comprehensive evaluation of calibration quality. Unlike classification where multiple metrics (ECE, MCE, Brier score) are reported, regression uncertainty is only shown visually for sine waves. There is no quantitative assessment of calibration quality on the head pose dataset or other regression tasks. This is particularly important given the paper's emphasis on uncertainty quantification as a key contribution. The paper should include quantitative calibration metrics for regression tasks to match the thoroughness of the classification analysis.

- Insufficient ablation study on SWA hyperparameters: The paper claims SWA is "robust to the choice of the hyperparameters" (Section 6) and references Appendix A.4, but the main text lacks detailed analysis of how SWA hyperparameters (learning rate, number of averaged epochs, start epoch) affect performance. Given that SWA is central to the paper's claims about low-rank representation, a more thorough ablation study is needed. The paper should show how performance varies with different SWA configurations and whether the low-rank property is consistent across these settings. This would strengthen the claim that SWA is the key driver of performance improvements.

- Missing worked example of low-rank representation computation: The paper claims SWA leads to low-rank representations but never shows a concrete computation of this property. A complete paper should include at least one worked example computing the effective rank for a specific model architecture (e.g., ResNet-12 on miniImageNet) both with and without SWA. This would involve showing the actual singular value decay curves, computing the effective rank metric described in Figure 2, and demonstrating how this correlates with few-shot performance on that specific task. Without such a concrete example, readers cannot properly evaluate the paper's central claim about SWA's mechanism.

- No demonstration of method on synthetic few-shot tasks: The paper evaluates on complex datasets but lacks a synthetic example that would clearly illustrate the method's behavior. For instance, a simple 2D synthetic task where the ground truth representation rank is known (e.g., data lying on a low-dimensional manifold) would allow readers to verify that MFRL indeed discovers the low-rank structure. This is particularly important because the paper's theoretical justification relies on the connection between SWA and low-rank representations. A synthetic demonstration would provide a controlled setting to validate this claim before moving to complex real-world datasets.

- Missing comparison with explicit low-rank regularizers: The paper claims SWA is advantageous because it's an implicit regularizer, but never compares against explicit low-rank regularization methods mentioned in Section 6 (e.g., nuclear norm, spectral norm). A complete evaluation should include experiments applying these explicit regularizers to the same transfer learning setup and comparing both accuracy and effective rank of representations. This would directly test whether SWA's benefits are truly due to low-rank regularization or other factors. Without this comparison, the paper cannot convincingly argue that SWA's implicit regularization is the key mechanism.

- No implementation guidance for hierarchical Bayesian fine-tuning: The paper describes using hierarchical Bayesian linear models for regression but provides no practical guidance on implementation details. Readers are left wondering about computational costs, convergence criteria, and sensitivity to hyperparameter choices. A complete paper should include a concrete example showing the fine-tuning process on a few-shot regression task, including computational time, number of iterations needed for convergence, and how the posterior uncertainty evolves. This is especially important given the paper's emphasis on uncertainty quantification as a key contribution.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide stronger theoretical justification for why SWA leads to low-rank representations that benefit few-shot learning

- Include comprehensive cross-domain experiments and analyze failure cases of the proposed method

- Clarify the novel contributions beyond combining existing techniques (SWA, Bayesian regression, temperature scaling)

- Add quantitative uncertainty calibration metrics for regression tasks to match the classification analysis

- Conduct thorough ablation studies on SWA hyperparameters and their impact on representation quality and performance

comments.title: Inconsistent weight matrix dimensions
comments.quote: For regression problems, the model learns $\mathcal{T}$ regression tasks ($\mathbf{W}=[\mathbf{w}_{1},...,\mathbf{w}_{\mathcal{T}}]\in\mathbb{R}^{(p+1)\times\mathcal{T}}$) simultaneously using the loss function $\mathcal{L}_{MSE}$ given in Eq. 2, whereas the model learns a $\mathcal{C}$-class classification model ($\mathbf{W}=[\mathbf{w}_{1},...,\mathbf{w}_{\mathcal{C}}]\in\mathbb{R}^{(p+1)\times\mathcal{C}}$) for classification problems using the loss function $\mathcal{L}_{CE}$ in Eq. 3.
comments.explanation: The paper specifies weight matrices as $(p+1)\times\mathcal{T}$ for regression and $(p+1)\times\mathcal{C}$ for classification, but the loss functions contradict this. In Eq. 2, the term $\mathbf{w}_{\tau}^{\top}h(\mathbf{x}_{\tau,j})$ requires $\mathbf{w}_{\tau}\in\mathbb{R}^{p}$ since $h(\mathbf{x}_{\tau,j})\in\mathbb{R}^{p}$. Similarly, Eq. 3 uses $\mathbf{w}_{c}^{\top}h(\mathbf{x}_{j})$ which also implies $\mathbf{w}_{c}\in\mathbb{R}^{p}$. The $(p+1)$ dimension would only be valid if bias terms were explicitly included in the computations, but they are not shown in either loss function. Rewrite the dimensions as $\mathbf{W}\in\mathbb{R}^{p\times\mathcal{T}}$ for regression and $\mathbf{W}\in\mathbb{R}^{p\times\mathcal{C}}$ for classification to match the actual loss function formulations.

comments.title: Logical gap in SWA to low-rank connection
comments.quote: As a result, SWA could result in a faster decay of eigenvalues in the kernel matrix, and thus low-rank representation. Our conjecture about SWA as implicit regularization towards low-rank representation is empirically verified in Section 5.
comments.explanation: The reasoning chain from SWA to low-rank representation has a gap. The paper establishes: SWA → flat minima (small Hessian eigenvalues) → Hessian ≈ Gauss-Newton → Gauss-Newton eigenvalues = NTK eigenvalues. However, it then makes an unsupported leap to conclude this implies fast decay of conjugate kernel eigenvalues. The paper only states that NTK and conjugate kernel eigenvalue distributions are 'empirically similar' without theoretical justification. This empirical similarity is insufficient to conclude that properties affecting NTK eigenvalues (like flat minima) will necessarily affect conjugate kernel eigenvalues in the same way. Add a theoretical justification for the relationship between NTK eigenvalue decay and conjugate kernel eigenvalue decay, or modify the claim to reflect that this connection is conjectural rather than theoretically established.

comments.title: Missing statistical significance for SWA improvement
comments.quote: In both few-shot regression problems, SWA results in improved accuracy, suggesting that SWA can improve the quality of features and facilitate the learning of downstream tasks.
comments.explanation: The claim that SWA improves accuracy is not statistically supported. For sine waves, MFRL improves from 0.023±0.016 to 0.016±0.008, but the confidence intervals overlap substantially (0.007 to 0.039 vs 0.008 to 0.024). Similarly for head pose, the improvement from 0.033±0.006 to 0.027±0.005 has overlapping intervals (0.027 to 0.039 vs 0.022 to 0.032). Without statistical significance testing, the claim that SWA 'results in improved accuracy' is not substantiated.

comments.title: Inconsistent uncertainty quantification claims
comments.quote:
ream tasks. In Fig. 1, uncertainty is correctly estimated by the hierarchical Bayesian linear model with learned features using just 10 training samples.

![img-0.jpeg](img-0.jpeg)
Figure 1: Sine wave regression and uncertainty quantification (10 training samples). The true and the estimated (by MFRL) standard deviation of data generation noise are 0.1, and
comments.explanation: The text claims uncertainty is 'correctly estimated' but the figure caption shows the estimated standard deviation (0.093) differs from the true value (0.1) by 7%. This discrepancy contradicts the claim of correct estimation. The authors should either qualify their claim (e.g., 'approximately correctly estimated') or provide quantitative metrics demonstrating calibration quality rather than relying on a single visual example with a noticeable error.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated computational cost claim
comments.quote: Compared with improving representation quality for few-shot classification via self-distillation (Tian et al., 2020), the computational cost of SWA is significantly smaller because it does not require training models from scratch multiple times.
comments.explanation: The paper claims SWA has significantly smaller computational cost than self-distillation but provides no concrete evidence. No training times, FLOPs comparisons, or wall-clock measurements are reported to support this claim. While the reasoning about not training models from scratch multiple times is logical, computational cost also depends on factors like SWA requiring multiple forward/backward passes for weight averaging, longer training schedules, and the specific implementation. Without quantitative evidence, this claim remains unsubstantiated. Add concrete computational metrics (training time, FLOPs, or GPU hours) comparing SWA versus distillation methods to support this claim.

comments.title: Missing justification for entropy metric
comments.quote: The metric  $-\sum \bar{\sigma}_i\log \bar{\sigma}_i$  is used to measure the effective rank of the representation, where  $\bar{\sigma}_i = \sigma_i / \sigma_{\mathrm{max}}$ . Faster decay in singular values indicates that fewer dimensions capture the most variation in all dimensions, thus lower effective rank.
comments.explanation: Add a brief explanation that this metric computes the Shannon entropy of the normalized singular value distribution, where lower entropy corresponds to more concentrated energy (faster decay) and thus lower effective rank. Also clarify that this metric ranges from 0 (rank-1) to log(n) (full rank) for n dimensions because the current text introduces the formula without explaining its theoretical foundation or interpretation.

comments.title: Undefined notation in classification loss
comments.quote: $\mathcal{L}_{CE}(\theta)$ $=-\sum_{j=1}^{N^{\prime}}\sum_{c=1}^{\mathcal{C}}y_{j,c}\log\frac{\exp(\mathbf{w}_{c}^{\top}h(\mathbf{x}_{j}))}{\sum_{c^{\prime}=1}^{\mathcal{C}}\exp(\mathbf{w}_{c^{\prime}}^{\top}h(\mathbf{x}_{j}))}$ (3)
comments.explanation: The classification loss in Eq. 3 uses notation $y_{j,c}$ without defining it. Given the context of cross-entropy loss, $y_{j,c}$ likely represents one-hot encoded labels (1 if sample $j$ belongs to class $c$, 0 otherwise), but this is not explicitly stated. Additionally, the summation index $j$ ranges from 1 to $N'$, but earlier $N'$ was defined as the total number of training samples across all tasks. For classification, it's unclear whether samples from different tasks share the same label space or how task-specific classification is handled in this aggregated formulation. Define $y_{j,c}$ explicitly as one-hot encoded labels and clarify how the classification loss handles multiple tasks with potentially different label spaces in the aggregated training setting.

comments.title: Temperature scaling accuracy claim needs qualification
comments.quote: Similarly, we do grid search of $T$ on the meta-validation set, and choose the temperature scaling factor resulting in the lowest expected calibration error *(Guo et al., 2017)*. Note that different values of $T$ do not affect the classification accuracy because temperature scaling is accuracy preserving.
comments.explanation: The claim that temperature scaling is accuracy preserving is too broad. Temperature scaling preserves the argmax prediction (the class with highest probability) for $T > 0$, but classification accuracy can depend on decision rules beyond argmax. The paper's own procedure of selecting $T$ based on validation data suggests different $T$ values affect model behavior. The statement should be qualified to specify that temperature scaling preserves the argmax prediction but may affect other aspects. Rewrite as: 'Note that different values of $T$ do not affect the argmax prediction because temperature scaling preserves the ordering of class probabilities.'

comments.title: Typo in baseline method name
comments.explanation: There is a clear typo in Table 2 where 'Basefine++ + SWA' should be 'Baseline++ + SWA' to be consistent with the other entries and the referenced method (Chen et al., 2019). This appears to be the SWA-augmented version of Baseline++ mentioned in the row above it. Rewrite 'Basefine++ + SWA' as 'Baseline++ + SWA' because this is a typographical error that creates inconsistency with the method naming throughout the paper.

comments.title: Typographical error in rank discussion
comments.quote: The rank of representation defines the number of independent bases. For deep learning, noise in gradients and numerical imprecision can cause the resulting matrix to be full-rank. Therefore, simply counting the number of non-zero singular values may not be an effective way to measure the rank of the representation. To compare the effective ranks, we plot the normalized singular values of the representation of meta-test data in Fig. 2, where the representation with SWA has a faster decay in singular values, thus indicating the lower effective rank of the presentation with SWA.
comments.explanation: The text contains a typographical error where 'presentation' is used instead of 'representation' in the final clause. Rewrite the final sentence as 'thus indicating the lower effective rank of the representation with SWA' because this appears to be a simple typographical error that should be corrected for clarity.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes Meta-Free Representation Learning (MFRL), a transfer learning approach for few-shot regression and classification that avoids episodic meta-learning. By utilizing Stochastic Weight Averaging (SWA) to regularize representations and fine-tuning probabilistic linear heads, the authors claim to achieve state-of-the-art performance and improved uncertainty calibration.

The paper provides a compelling argument for moving away from complex meta-learning paradigms toward simpler, robust transfer learning methods. The empirical results on standard benchmarks are strong, and the integration of uncertainty calibration through temperature scaling and Bayesian linear models is a welcome addition to the few-shot literature. However, the theoretical justification for SWA as an implicit regularizer toward low-rank representation remains speculative and requires more rigorous validation.

Overview issues:

- Theoretical justification for SWA as a low-rank regularizer: In Section 4.1, the authors conjecture that SWA acts as an implicit regularizer toward low-rank representation, linking it to the decay of eigenvalues in the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK). This connection is purely heuristic and lacks a formal derivation or a clear link between the geometry of the loss landscape at convergence and the rank of the learned feature map. Simply observing faster singular value decay in Figure 2 does not prove that SWA is the causal mechanism for this rank reduction, nor does it address whether this is a general property or an artifact of the specific architectures used. To strengthen this claim, the authors should provide a more rigorous analysis or at least perform an ablation study comparing SWA against explicit low-rank regularizers (e.g., nuclear norm penalties) to see if they produce similar performance gains and spectral properties.

- Data leakage and pre-training overlap: A recurring concern in meta-free transfer learning is the potential for data leakage between the large-scale pre-training dataset and the downstream meta-test tasks. While the authors evaluate on standard benchmarks, they do not explicitly detail whether the pre-training datasets (e.g., ImageNet) contain classes that overlap with the test sets of miniImageNet or tieredImageNet. If the feature extractor was trained on images that appear in the test set, the reported accuracy improvements might be inflated. The paper needs a clear statement on the overlap between pre-training and test sets, and ideally, an evaluation on a truly disjoint domain to confirm the robustness of the learned representations.

- Sensitivity to hyperparameter selection in the meta-test phase: The methodology in Section 4.2 relies on grid searching for the regularization coefficient lambda and the temperature scaling factor T on meta-validation data. While this is common practice, the paper claims that MFRL is robust, yet it does not sufficiently explore the sensitivity of the final performance to these parameters when the meta-validation set is small or distributionally shifted. Given that few-shot tasks are defined by their low-data nature, the reliance on a separate validation set for hyperparameter tuning might not be feasible in real-world deployments. A more detailed sensitivity analysis or a discussion on how to select these parameters without a large validation set would significantly improve the practical utility of the proposed method.

- Computational overhead vs. performance gains: The authors argue that MFRL is more efficient than meta-learning, yet the SWA process requires running additional training epochs after initial convergence (Eq. 1). The paper lacks a direct comparison of the total computational budget (pre-training time + SWA time + fine-tuning time) against standard fine-tuning or meta-learning baselines. Without this, the claim of efficiency is incomplete. Including a table comparing total training time and GPU hours for the different methods would allow readers to better assess whether the performance gains from SWA are worth the extra computational cost.

- Lack of empirical validation for low-rank regularization hypothesis: The authors hypothesize that SWA acts as an implicit regularizer toward low-rank representation, yet the evidence provided is purely observational based on singular value decay. To establish this as a core contribution, the paper should compare the spectral properties of representations learned via SWA against those learned using an explicit low-rank penalty, such as a nuclear norm constraint on the feature extractor weights. If SWA is truly a proxy for low-rank regularization, the performance gains and spectral decay patterns should be qualitatively similar to those obtained with explicit rank-minimizing objectives. This would move the claim from a post-hoc observation to a verifiable property of the learning paradigm.

- Missing computational budget analysis for the SWA phase: While the paper claims MFRL is efficient, it neglects a direct comparison of the total computational budget required for SWA versus standard fine-tuning or meta-learning baselines. The SWA process requires running additional training epochs, which adds to the training overhead. To substantiate the efficiency claim, the paper should include a table reporting the total wall-clock time or GPU hours for training, including the SWA phase, compared to baseline methods like MAML or standard fine-tuning. Without this, the reader cannot determine if the performance improvements are cost-effective relative to the added training time.

- Inadequate discussion on hyperparameter sensitivity in low-data regimes: The methodology relies on grid searching for the regularization coefficient and temperature scaling factor on a meta-validation set. This approach assumes the existence of a representative validation set, which is often unavailable in real-world few-shot scenarios. The paper would benefit from a sensitivity analysis showing how performance degrades when the meta-validation set size is reduced or when there is a distribution shift between the validation and test tasks. Providing guidance on how to select these parameters in the absence of a large validation set would bridge the gap between the proposed method and its stated practical utility.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Provide a more rigorous theoretical or empirical argument for the causal link between SWA and low-rank representation, possibly by comparing SWA to explicit low-rank regularization.

- Include a clear disclosure or analysis regarding potential data leakage between pre-training and test datasets to ensure the validity of the reported accuracy.

- Add a computational complexity analysis comparing the total training time of MFRL against the baseline meta-learning methods used in the experiments.

- Perform a sensitivity analysis of the hyperparameters (lambda and T) to demonstrate the robustness of the method when validation data is limited or non-representative.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim regarding uncertainty quantification
comments.quote: Extensive experiments show that our method not only outperforms other SOTA methods on various datasets but also correctly quantifies the uncertainty in prediction.
comments.explanation: The paper claims to correctly quantify uncertainty, yet the methodology relies on temperature scaling, which is a heuristic for confidence calibration rather than a rigorous method for uncertainty quantification. The formal sections do not define an objective function or provide a theoretical guarantee for uncertainty estimation. Please remove the claim that the method 'correctly quantifies' uncertainty unless you provide evidence or a formal definition of the uncertainty measure being used.

comments.title: Inconsistency in effective rank definition
comments.quote: The metric  $-\sum \bar{\sigma}_i\log \bar{\sigma}_i$  is used to measure the effective rank of the representation, where  $\bar{\sigma}_i = \sigma_i / \sigma_{\mathrm{max}}$ .
comments.explanation: The expression provided is the Shannon entropy of the normalized singular value distribution, which measures the spread of the spectrum rather than the dimension of the representation. This is not a standard definition of effective rank. Please define the metric as a 'spectral entropy proxy' or use a dimension-consistent measure such as the stable rank (nuclear norm divided by spectral norm) to avoid confusion.

comments.title: Inconsistency in weight matrix dimension and dot product notation
comments.quote: For regression problems, the model learns $\mathcal{T}$ regression tasks ($\mathbf{W}=\left[\mathbf{w}_{1},...,\mathbf{w}_{\mathcal{T}}\right]\in\mathbb{R}^{(p+1)\times\mathcal{T}}$) simultaneously using the loss function $\mathcal{L}_{MSE}$ given in Eq. 2
comments.explanation: The weight matrix $\mathbf{W}$ is defined as $(p+1) \times \mathcal{T}$, which includes a bias term, but the loss functions in Eq. 2 and 3 use $\mathbf{w}^{\top}h(\mathbf{x})$, which is a dot product between a vector of dimension $p+1$ and a vector of dimension $p$. This is mathematically undefined. Please rewrite the loss functions to use $\mathbf{w}^{\top} [h(\mathbf{x}); 1]$ or adjust the dimensions of $\mathbf{W}$ to $p \times \mathcal{T}$ if no bias is intended.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Gauss-Newton matrix definition
comments.quote: In parallel, $K^{\mathrm{NTK}}$ shares the same eigenvalues of the Gauss-Newton matrix $G=\frac{1}{N^{\prime}}\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{X})^{\top}\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{X})$.
comments.explanation: The Gauss-Newton matrix $G$ is defined as $\frac{1}{N^{\prime}}\mathbf{J}^{\top}\mathbf{J}$, which is a $|\theta| \times |\theta|$ matrix, whereas the NTK $K^{\mathrm{NTK}} = \mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^{\top}$ is an $N^{\prime} \times N^{\prime}$ matrix. While they share non-zero eigenvalues, they are not the same matrix. Please clarify that this refers only to the non-zero spectrum to avoid implying the matrices are identical.

comments.title: Inconsistent summation index definition
comments.quote: where $\theta_{i}$ denotes the model parameters at the end of the $i$-th epoch.
